questions.question: Under the Atomic Energy Act, the depleted-uranium shield inside a gamma exposure device and the Ir-192 source it shields are classified, respectively, as:
options: Byproduct material and special nuclear material
Source material and byproduct material
Special nuclear material and source material
Neither — only the assembled device is licensed, not the materials inside it
questions.explanation: AEA authority follows the material: uranium and thorium are source material, so the depleted-uranium shield is licensed as source material, while reactor-activated isotopes like Ir-192, Co-60, and Se-75 are byproduct material. Special nuclear material — plutonium and enriched uranium — plays no role in radiography. Both the shield and the source appear on the materials license.

questions.question: An Agreement State licensee authorized for temporary job sites takes its first reciprocity job of the year in a non-Agreement State. Which statement about working under 10 CFR 150.20 is correct?
options: Filing NRC Form 241 with a license copy and fee at least 3 days before work activates an NRC general license, capped at 180 days of work in NRC jurisdiction per calendar year
No filing is needed — an Agreement State license is automatically valid in any non-Agreement State
Each Form 241 filed grants its own fresh 180-day allowance
Reciprocity moves the radiographer's personal IRRSP certification, which substitutes for any company filing
questions.explanation: Reciprocity is activated by filing, never by assumption: Form 241, a copy of the license, and the fee go in at least 3 days ahead, with revisions when sites or dates change. The 180-day ceiling is an aggregate cap per calendar year across all NRC-jurisdiction work — it does not reset per filing. Reciprocity moves the company license; personal certification never substitutes for it.

questions.question: Before issuing a specific industrial radiography license, the regulator reviews the NRC Form 313 application against the general standards of 10 CFR 30.33, which require among other things that:
options: The company posts a financial bond guaranteeing public dose limits will never be exceeded
The company employs at least one ASNT NDT Level III in radiographic testing
The applicant's equipment and facilities are adequate to protect health, and the applicant is qualified by training and experience to use the material safely
The applicant has first operated for one year under a general license
questions.explanation: Industrial radiography may be conducted only under a specific license — no general license covers it, and there is no probationary general-license year. Under 10 CFR 30.33 the regulator must find the use authorized by the Act, the equipment and facilities adequate, the applicant qualified by training and experience, and the special requirements of Part 34 satisfied. Bonds and NDT Level III certificates are not licensing standards.

questions.question: Which statement about living under a radioactive materials license is correct?
options: A new exposure-device model may be placed in service immediately, with the license updated at the next renewal
A renewal application filed at least 30 days before expiration keeps the existing license in effect under the timely-renewal rule until the regulator acts
The license transfers automatically to the buyer when the company is sold
Replacing the named RSO is an internal personnel matter requiring no regulatory action
questions.explanation: Timely renewal — filed at least 30 days before expiration — holds the license in effect until the regulator decides. Everything else runs the other direction: a new device model, a new RSO, more activity, or a new location requires an amendment before the change, and the license is not transferable without the regulator's prior written consent.

questions.question: A company complies fully with its Agreement State license for its Ir-192 program and also runs X-ray crawler jobs. With respect to OSHA's ionizing radiation standard, 29 CFR 1910.1096, the company is:
options: Deemed in compliance for all of its radiation work, machines included
Required to keep a separate set of OSHA quarterly dose records for the gamma crews
Exempt from every OSHA requirement on its job sites
Deemed in compliance only for the licensed material — the crawler holds no licensed material, so machine work still answers to 1910.1096 and the state's machine rules
questions.explanation: Paragraph (p) of 1910.1096 deems an NRC or Agreement State licensee complying with its regulator's standards to be in compliance with OSHA's radiation rule — but only for the possession and use of the licensed material. Machine-produced radiation is outside the license, so it stays with OSHA and the state machine program, and conventional hazards like falls and confined spaces remain fully OSHA's everywhere.

questions.question: Before a new hire may operate an exposure device as a radiographer's assistant, 10 CFR 34.43(b) and 34.46 require:
options: Instruction in the governing documents, a written test (an oral exam does not qualify), a practical examination on the hardware used, and personal supervision by a radiographer who is present, available to assist immediately, and watching
An IRRSP certification card in the gamma category before any source handling
A written or oral examination at the licensee's choice, plus a radiographer reachable by phone during operations
No formal requirements — assistants qualify automatically after 90 days of field observation
questions.explanation: The assistant path deliberately skips the certification card, but nothing else: instruction in the same governing documents, a written test with no oral alternative, and a practical exam on the actual equipment. Personal supervision then has three required elements — the radiographer physically at the site, available for immediate assistance, and watching the operation. A phone link fails all three.

questions.question: Which set of intervals correctly maintains personnel qualification under 10 CFR 34.43 and 34.79?
options: Refresher training every 6 months, performance audits every 12 months, records kept 1 year
Refresher training every 24 months, with performance audits satisfied by an annual office paperwork review
Refresher training at intervals not exceeding 12 months, performance observed during an actual radiographic operation at intervals not exceeding 6 months, records kept 3 years
Refresher training and performance audits both every 5 years, matching the IRRSP card term
questions.explanation: The maintenance clocks are 12 months for refresher safety training and 6 months for the RSO or designee to observe each radiographer and assistant during real radiographic work — a desk review does not satisfy it. If someone has not participated for more than 6 months since the last inspection, they are observed the next time they work. Records of it all are kept for 3 years under 34.79.

questions.question: Your pre-shipment survey of a loaded exposure device reads a maximum of 90 mrem/h at the package surface and 2.2 mrem/h at 1 meter. The correct label is:
options: Yellow-II with TI = 2.2, because the surface reading stays under 200 mrem/h
Yellow-III with TI = 2.2 — the 90 mrem/h surface reading exceeds the 50 mrem/h Yellow-II limit, and the TI of 2.2 exceeds 1
Yellow-III with TI = 90, using the higher of the two readings
White-I, because the device shield keeps the source contained
questions.explanation: A package must satisfy both criteria of a category: Yellow-II allows up to 50 mrem/h at the surface and a TI of 1 or less, and this package busts both. Yellow-III covers it (surface up to 200 mrem/h, TI up to 10). The TI is always the 1-meter reading rounded up to the next tenth — never the surface value — and with a Yellow-III package aboard, the vehicle wears RADIOACTIVE placards on all four sides.

questions.question: After enduring the hypothetical accident sequence — the 30-foot drop, the puncture bar, the 1,475 °F fire, and immersion — a Type B package must still:
options: Show no visible damage of any kind
Read no more than 0.5 mrem/h at the package surface
Float, so the source can be recovered from water
Contain its contents, retain its shielding, and read no more than 1 rem/h at 1 meter
questions.explanation: Type B certification proves accident survival, not cosmetic integrity: after the test sequence the package must keep its contents in, keep its shielding working, and read no more than 1 rem/h at 1 meter. The 0.5 mrem/h figure is the White-I surface label limit, and the water-spray and four-foot drop belong to the gentler Type A normal-transport tests. A working radiography source above its A1 value — 1 TBq (27 Ci) for Ir-192 — must ride in an NRC-certified Type B design.

questions.question: A crew drives home with a de-energized 250 kV X-ray crawler aboard and no radioactive sources on the truck. Which DOT Class 7 controls apply?
options: None — a de-energized tube emits nothing and contains no radioactive material, so it ships as ordinary freight
A Yellow-II label and shipping papers, because the tube can produce radiation
RADIOACTIVE placards on all four sides of the vehicle
A Type A package meeting DOT Specification 7A
questions.explanation: Class 7 follows radioactive material, and an X-ray tube holds none — switched off, it emits nothing, so no labels, placards, papers, or specification packaging apply. The machine's regulatory life is with the state radiation control program through registration, with FDA manufacturer standards under 21 CFR 1020; a gamma camera on the same truck would trigger the full DOT regime.
